RT @Slatzism: At risk of getting yeeted for QRTing the King, I think his post here gets to the very crux of the debate.

When someone invokes Nikola Tesla as an example of why immigration is good, they are communicating a belief that immigration can uniformly produce the same results…

As though the immigrant himself matters less than the process, or that the process itself contributes to the formative development of the immigrant.

Immigration is not responsible for Nikola Tesla's greatness. Nikola Tesla was a genius before he got to the US, and would have been a genius had he never went (and btw, he notably wrote about how much he regretted migrating to the US instead of Russia).

Crediting immigration or even magic American soil for Nikola Tesla is an easy way of avoiding the more offensive fact that he, like most great immigrants, was a very specific product of his birth, upbringing, culture, values, resources, genetics, and zeitgeist as an inimitable whole.

You can meatgrind 100,000 computer programers from Bangalore through the same process all you want, they are not and will never be Teslas.

What so many on the "techright" seem to be doing right now is a sort of Ship of Theseus thought experiment but instead of Theseus' ship to Delos, it's a boat to Ellis Island with Nikola Tesla on it.

Or, perhaps better stated, it’s like a gambling addiction.

Their fingers are bloody and raw after rubbing through millions of scratchcards without a penny in winnings but they can’t stop because they haven’t hit the jackpot yet and WHAT IF.

They can’t pull the plug on this insurmountably flawed system because WHAT IF the next Nikola Tesla is a guy from Honduras in the process of obtaining an H1B to work on a hog farm in Laurinburg, North Carolina?
